{{表記揺れ案内|表記1=豊鹿嶋神社|表記2=豊鹿島神社|表記3=|議論ページ=}} {{Shrine | Name = Toyokashima Shrine | image = [[File:豊鹿嶋神社正面より.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, {{ill|Higashiyamato, Tokyo|ja|東大和市}} Imokubo 1-2067<ref name="cho"/> | coordinates = {{Coord|35|45|28.5|N|139|24|46.2|E|type:landmark_region:JP-13}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命}} | Status = {{ill|Gōsha (Shinto)|ja|郷社}} | Established = 1466 | Festival = | Map = Japan Tokyo }}
'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashiyamato, Tokyo|ja|東大和市}}, Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, is the oldest existing {{ill|Shinto shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ({{ill|Keiun|ja|慶雲}} 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国}} was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}''' and {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂}}<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher={{ill|Higashiyamato City|ja|東大和市}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ({{ill|Heisei era|ja|平成}} 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ({{ill|Bunshō|ja|文正}} 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ({{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education|ja|東京都教育委員会}}|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ({{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}}) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher={{ill|Higashiyamato City|ja|東大和市}}|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ({{ill|Shishimai|ja|獅子舞}}) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧}}

* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ({{ill|Shishimai|ja|獅子舞}}) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.

revision.text:
{{表記揺れ案内|表記1=豊鹿嶋神社|表記2=豊鹿島神社|表記3=|議論ページ=}} {{Shrine | Name = Toyokashima Shrine | image = [[File:豊鹿嶋神社正面より.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, {{ill|Higashiyamato, Tokyo|ja|東大和市}} Imokubo 1-2067<ref name="cho"/> | coordinates = {{Coord|35|45|28.5|N|139|24|46.2|E|type:landmark_region:JP-13}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命}} | Status = {{ill|Gōsha (Shinto)|ja|郷社}} | Established = 1466 | Festival = | Map = Japan Tokyo }}
'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashiyamato, Tokyo|ja|東大和市}}, Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, is the oldest existing {{ill|Shinto shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ({{ill|Keiun|ja|慶雲}} 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国}} was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}}''' and {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂}}<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher={{ill|Higashiyamato City|ja|東大和市}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ({{ill|Heisei era|ja|平成}} 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ({{ill|Bunshō|ja|文正}} 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ({{ill|Ansei|ja|安政}} 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education|ja|東京都教育委員会}}|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ({{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}}) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher={{ill|Higashiyamato City|ja|東大和市}}|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ({{ill|Shishimai|ja|獅子舞}}) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧}}

revision.text:
{{表記揺れ案内|表記1=豊鹿嶋神社|表記2=豊鹿島神社|表記3=|議論ページ=}} {{Shrine | Name = Toyokashima Shrine | image = [[File:豊鹿嶋神社正面より.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都|12=draft}}, {{ill|Higashiyamato, Tokyo|ja|東大和市|12=draft}} Imokubo 1-2067<ref name="cho"/> | coordinates = {{Coord|35|45|28.5|N|139|24|46.2|E|type:landmark_region:JP-13}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|12=draft}} | Status = {{ill|Gōsha (Shinto)|ja|郷社|12=draft}} | Established = 1466 | Festival = | Map = Japan Tokyo }}
'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Higashiyamato, Tokyo|ja|東大和市|12=draft}}, Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|12=draft}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|12=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, is the oldest existing {{ill|Shinto shrine architecture|ja|神社建築|12=draft}} in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ({{ill|Keiun|ja|慶雲|12=draft}} 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|12=draft}} was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇|12=draft}}''' and {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂|12=draft}}<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|12=draft}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|12=draft}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher={{ill|Higashiyamato City|ja|東大和市|12=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ({{ill|Heisei era|ja|平成|12=draft}} 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ({{ill|Bunshō|ja|文正|12=draft}} 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ({{ill|Ansei|ja|安政|12=draft}} 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education|ja|東京都教育委員会|12=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ({{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}}) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|12=draft}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=draft}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher={{ill|Higashiyamato City|ja|東大和市|12=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ({{ill|Shishimai|ja|獅子舞|12=draft}}) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|12=draft}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|12=draft}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Tokyo-designated tangible cultural properties]] [[Category:Municipal cultural properties in Tokyo]] [[Category:Higashiyamato, Tokyo]] [[Category:東京都の神社]] [[Category:東京都の旧郷社]] [[Category:東京都指定有形文化財]] [[Category:東京都内の区市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:東大和市の建築物]] [[Category:東大和市の歴史]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[ja:豊鹿嶋神社]] [[sn:oyokashima Shrine]] [[sn:Toyokashima Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Toyokashima Shrine}}

revision.text:
{{表記揺れ案内|表記1=豊鹿嶋神社|表記2=豊鹿島神社|表記3=|議論ページ=}} {{神社 |名称=豊鹿嶋神社 |画像=[[File:豊鹿嶋神社正面より.jpg|300px]] |所在地=[[東京都]][[東大和市]]芋窪1-2067<ref name="cho"/> |位置=<!--{{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|45|28.5|N|139|24|46.2|E|scale:30000|display=title}}--> |祭神=武御加豆智命 |社格=[[郷社]] |創建=1466年 |例祭= |地図=Japan Tokyo|緯度度=35|緯度分=45|緯度秒=28.5|経度度=139|経度分=24|経度秒=46.2}} '''豊鹿嶋神社'''（とよかしまじんじゃ）とは、[[東京都]][[東大和市]]芋窪にある[[神社]]。祭神は[[タケミカヅチ|武御加豆智命]]<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher=[[神社庁|東京都神社庁]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>。本殿は[[室町時代]]の[[1466年]]（[[文正]]元年）建立であり、現存する東京都最古の[[神社建築]]である<ref name="cho"/>。
== 歴史 == 社伝によれば、[[707年]]（[[慶雲]]4年）、[[武蔵国]]に来た[[鬼|鬼神]]を常陸峯に鎮めて、[[天智天皇]]の第四の姫宮と[[蘇我倉山田石川麻呂|蘇我山田石川麿]]が建立したことに始まる<ref name="book">{{Cite book |和書 |author=植田孟縉|authorlink=植田孟縉|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|location= |isbn=4874490808}}</ref>。以来、芋窪地域の鎮守として住民により守られてきた<ref name="cho"/>。
[[江戸時代]]には13石の[[社領]]を与えられていた<ref name="city">{{Cite web|和書|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=東大和市|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>。
[[1993年]]（[[平成]]5年）に行われた[[拝殿]]の大改修において、『[[新編武蔵風土記稿]]』に記載されていた通り{{sfn|新編武蔵風土記稿 芋久保村 鹿島神社}}、[[文正]]元年[[10月3日]]の[[棟札]]が発見され、創建年が実証された。また、この改修で計5枚の棟札が発見され、改修前の拝殿は[[1858年]]（[[安政]]5年）に完成したことが確認された<ref name="book"/>。
== 文化財 == ; 東京都指定有形文化財（建造物） * 豊鹿島神社本殿 1棟 附・棟札 1枚 - 1964年（昭和39年）11月21日に都重宝として指定、1976年（昭和51年）7月1日に種別変更<ref name="to">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=東京都文化財情報データベース|publisher=[[東京都教育委員会|東京都教育庁]]地域教育支援部|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : [[流造|一間社流造]]の本殿で[[こけら葺]]箱棟付、桁行（間口）は170.7cm、身舎の梁間（奥行）は152.4cm、向拝の梁間は140.4cmである<ref name="to"/>。現状は外気や風雨から保護するための[[覆堂|覆殿]]の中にあり、外から観察することはできない<ref name="cho"/>。建築年代について、かつては本殿内に保管されていた棟札にあった年紀通りに1550年（[[天文 (元号)|天文]]19年）とされてきたが、1992年（平成4年）から2年に及ぶ解体修理の際、大工二郎三郎近吉が1466年（文正元年）に建立した旨を記した棟札が新たに発見され、また、古くは厚板葺の屋根であったことも分かった<ref name="to"/>。
; 東大和市指定文化財（市重宝） * 豊鹿島神社本殿の木製[[狛犬]] - 1983年（昭和58年）3月1日指定<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|和書|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=東大和市|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : 木製、本殿と同時期に作られた可能性が高いとされる<ref name="bunka"/>。 * 豊鹿島神社の[[獅子頭]] - 1983年（昭和58年）3月1日指定<ref name="bunka"/> : 江戸時代後期の作か、かつて奉納されていた[[獅子舞]]に用いられたとみられる<ref name="bunka"/>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == * {{cite book|和書|title=新編武蔵風土記稿|volume=巻ノ120多磨郡ノ32|chapter=芋久保村 鹿島神社|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher=内務省地理局|date=1884-6|ref={{sfnref|新編武蔵風土記稿 芋久保村 鹿島神社}}}}
== 関連項目 == * [[東京都の神社一覧]] * [[東京都指定文化財一覧]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:とよかしましんしや}} [[Category:鹿島神社]] [[Category:東京都の神社]] [[Category:東京都の旧郷社]] [[Category:東京都指定有形文化財]] [[Category:東京都内の区市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:東大和市の建築物]] [[Category:東大和市の歴史]]

revision.text:
{{表記揺れ案内|表記1=豊鹿嶋神社|表記2=豊鹿島神社|表記3=|議論ページ=}} {{Shrine | Name = Toyokashima Shrine | image = [[File:豊鹿嶋神社正面より.jpg|300px]] | location = {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Higashiyamato, Tokyo|ja|東大和市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Imokubo 1-2067<ref name="cho"/> | coordinates = {{Coord|35|45|28.5|N|139|24|46.2|E|type:landmark_region:JP-13}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Status = {{ill|Gōsha (Shinto)|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Established = 1466 | Festival = | Map = Japan Tokyo }}
'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Higashiyamato, Tokyo|ja|東大和市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is the oldest existing {{ill|Shinto shrine architecture|ja|神社建築|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ({{ill|Keiun|ja|慶雲|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' and {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|qq=draft}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher={{ill|Higashiyamato City|ja|東大和市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ({{ill|Heisei era|ja|平成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ({{ill|Bunshō|ja|文正|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ({{ill|Ansei|ja|安政|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education|ja|東京都教育委員会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ({{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher={{ill|Higashiyamato City|ja|東大和市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ({{ill|Shishimai|ja|獅子舞|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Toyokashima Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|豊鹿嶋神社|version=98289402|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Tokyo-designated tangible cultural properties]] [[Category:Municipal cultural properties in Tokyo]] [[Category:Higashiyamato, Tokyo]] [[Category:東京都の神社]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:東京都指定有形文化財]] [[Category:東京都内の区市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:東大和市の建築物]] [[Category:東大和市の歴史]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:New]] }}

Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|qq=draft}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher={{ill|Higashiyamato City|ja|東大和市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ({{ill|Heisei era|ja|平成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ({{ill|Bunshō|ja|文正|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ({{ill|Ansei|ja|安政|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education|ja|東京都教育委員会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ({{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher={{ill|Higashiyamato City|ja|東大和市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ({{ill|Shishimai|ja|獅子舞|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.

'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Higashiyamato, Tokyo|ja|東大和市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, is the oldest existing {{ill|Shinto shrine architecture|ja|神社建築|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.

According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ({{ill|Keiun|ja|慶雲|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' and {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|qq=draft}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher={{ill|Higashiyamato City|ja|東大和市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ({{ill|Heisei era|ja|平成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ({{ill|Bunshō|ja|文正|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). In '''1993''' ({{ill|Heisei era|ja|平成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ({{ill|Bunshō|ja|文正|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ({{ill|Ansei|ja|安政|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education|ja|東京都教育委員会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ({{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher={{ill|Higashiyamato City|ja|東大和市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ({{ill|Shishimai|ja|獅子舞|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Higashiyamato, Tokyo|ja|東大和市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyamato, Tokyo|qq=}}, Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, is the oldest existing {{ill|Shinto shrine architecture|ja|神社建築|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine architecture|qq=}} in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ({{ill|Keiun|ja|慶雲|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keiun|qq=}} 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Province|qq=}} was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenji|qq=}}''' and {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|ja|蘇我倉山田石川麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|qq=}}<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|qq=}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|qq=}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher={{ill|Higashiyamato City|ja|東大和市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyamato City|qq=}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ({{ill|Heisei era|ja|平成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei era|qq=}} 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ({{ill|Bunshō|ja|文正|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunshō|qq=}} 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ({{ill|Ansei|ja|安政|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ansei|qq=}} 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education|ja|東京都教育委員会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education|qq=}}|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ({{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagare-zukuri|qq=}}) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|qq=}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher={{ill|Higashiyamato City|ja|東大和市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashiyamato City|qq=}}|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ({{ill|Shishimai|ja|獅子舞|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shishimai|qq=}}) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.

The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. {{表記揺れ案内|表記1=豊鹿嶋神社|表記2=豊鹿島神社|表記3=|議論ページ=}} {{Shrine | Name = Toyokashima Shrine | image = [[File:豊鹿嶋神社正面より.jpg|300px]] | location = [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]] Imokubo 1-2067<ref name="cho"/> | coordinates = {{Coord|35|45|28.5|N|139|24|46.2|E|type:landmark_region:JP-13}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=}} | Status = {{ill|Gōsha (Shinto)|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha (Shinto)|qq=}} | Established = 1466 | Festival = | Map = Japan Tokyo }}
'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]], Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|qq=}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|qq=}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|qq=}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|qq=}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|qq=}}

'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]], Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|qq=}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|qq=}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|qq=}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|qq=}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|qq=}}

The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|qq=}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|qq=}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|qq=}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|qq=}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|qq=}}

The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|qq=}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|qq=}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.

== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|qq=}}

The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|qq=}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|qq=}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|qq=}}

The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|qq=}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|qq=}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|qq=}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q85883840]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{表記揺れ案内|表記1=豊鹿嶋神社|表記2=豊鹿島神社|表記3=|議論ページ=}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Toyokashima Shrine | image = File:豊鹿嶋神社正面より.jpg | location = [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]] Imokubo 1-2067<ref name="cho"/> | coordinates = {{Coord|35.75791666666667|N|139.41283333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | Status = {{ill|Gōsha (Shinto)|ja|郷社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#District_shrine|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha (Shinto)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} | Established = 1466 | Festival = | Map = Japan Tokyo | location = {{ill|Higashiyamato|qid=Q390788}} }}
'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]], Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社庁}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|qq=}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|qq=}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
