revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q85883840]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{表記揺れ案内|表記1=豊鹿嶋神社|表記2=豊鹿島神社|表記3=|議論ページ=}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Toyokashima Shrine | image = File:豊鹿嶋神社正面より.jpg | location = [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]] Imokubo 1-2067<ref name="cho"/> | coordinates = {{Coord|35.75791666666667|N|139.41283333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | Status = {{ill|Gōsha (Shinto)|ja|郷社|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha (Shinto)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Established = 1466 | Festival = | Map = Japan Tokyo | location = {{ill|Higashiyamato|qid=Q390788}} }}
'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]], Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社庁|1=Jinja-cho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590703}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|qq=|1=Ueda Mōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540684}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|qq=|1=fukudo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630077}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|qq=|1=list of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525192}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|qq=|1=List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525325}}
{{translated page|ja|豊鹿嶋神社|version=98289402|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|qq=|1=fukudo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630077}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|qq=|1=list of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525192}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|qq=|1=List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525325}}

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q85883840]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{表記揺れ案内|表記1=豊鹿嶋神社|表記2=豊鹿島神社|表記3=|議論ページ=}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Toyokashima Shrine | image = File:豊鹿嶋神社正面より.jpg | location = [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]] Imokubo 1-2067<ref name="cho"/> | coordinates = {{Coord|35.75791666666667|N|139.41283333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | Status = {{ill|Gōsha (Shinto)|ja|郷社|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha (Shinto)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Established = 1466 | Festival = | Map = Japan Tokyo | location = {{ill|Higashiyamato|qid=Q390788}} }}
'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]], Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社庁|1=Jinja-cho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590703}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|qq=|1=Ueda Mōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540684}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|qq=|1=fukudo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630077}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|qq=|1=list of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525192}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|qq=|1=List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525325}}

'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]], Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社庁|1=Jinja-cho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590703}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|qq=|1=Ueda Mōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540684}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|qq=|1=fukudo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630077}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|qq=|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:豊鹿嶋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q85883840]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{表記揺れ案内|表記1=豊鹿嶋神社|表記2=豊鹿島神社|表記3=|議論ページ=}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Toyokashima Shrine | image = File:豊鹿嶋神社正面より.jpg | location = [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]] Imokubo 1-2067<ref name="cho"/> | coordinates = {{Coord|35.75791666666667|N|139.41283333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | Status = {{ill|Gōsha (Shinto)|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha (Shinto)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Established = 1466 | Festival = | Map = Japan Tokyo | location = {{ill|Higashiyamato|WD=Q390788}} }}
'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]], Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社庁|1=Jinja-cho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590703}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|1=Ueda Mōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540684}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|1=fukudo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630077}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|1=fukudo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630077}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:豊鹿嶋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q85883840]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{表記揺れ案内|表記1=豊鹿嶋神社|表記2=豊鹿島神社|表記3=|議論ページ=}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Toyokashima Shrine | image = File:豊鹿嶋神社正面より.jpg | location = [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]] Imokubo 1-2067<ref name="cho"/> | coordinates = {{Coord|35.75791666666667|N|139.41283333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | Status = {{ill|Gōsha (Shinto)|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha (Shinto)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Established = 1466 | Festival = | Map = Japan Tokyo | location = {{ill|Higashiyamato|WD=Q390788}} }}
'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]], Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社庁|1=Jinja-cho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590703}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|1=Ueda Mōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540684}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|1=fukudo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630077}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.

The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|1=Ueda Mōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540684}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|1=fukudo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630077}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:豊鹿嶋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q85883840]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{表記揺れ案内|表記1=豊鹿嶋神社|表記2=豊鹿島神社|表記3=|議論ページ=}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Toyokashima Shrine | image = File:豊鹿嶋神社正面より.jpg | location = [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]] Imokubo 1-2067<ref name="cho"/> | coordinates = {{Coord|35.75791666666667|N|139.41283333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | Status = {{ill|Gōsha (Shinto)|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha (Shinto)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Established = 1466 | Festival = | Map = Japan Tokyo | location = {{ill|Higashiyamato|WD=Q390788}} }}
'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]], Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社庁|1=Jinja-cho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590703}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|1=Ueda Mōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540684}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|1=fukudo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630077}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|1=list of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525192}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|1=List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525325}}
{{translated page|ja|豊鹿嶋神社|version=98289402|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|1=fukudo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630077}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|1=list of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525192}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|1=List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525325}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:豊鹿嶋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q85883840]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{表記揺れ案内|表記1=豊鹿嶋神社|表記2=豊鹿島神社|表記3=|議論ページ=}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Toyokashima Shrine | image = File:豊鹿嶋神社正面より.jpg | location = [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]] Imokubo 1-2067<ref name="cho"/> | coordinates = {{Coord|35.75791666666667|N|139.41283333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | Status = {{ill|Gōsha (Shinto)|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha (Shinto)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Established = 1466 | Festival = | Map = Japan Tokyo | location = {{ill|Higashiyamato|WD=Q390788}} }}
'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]], Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社庁|1=Jinja-cho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590703}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|1=Ueda Mōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540684}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|1=fukudo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630077}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|1=list of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525192}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|1=List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525325}}

'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]], Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社庁|1=Jinja-cho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590703}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|1=Ueda Mōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540684}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|1=fukudo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630077}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|1=list of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525192}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|1=List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525325}}

revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:豊鹿嶋神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:豊鹿嶋神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:豊鹿嶋神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q85883840]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{表記揺れ案内|表記1=豊鹿嶋神社|表記2=豊鹿島神社|表記3=|議論ページ=}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name = Toyokashima Shrine | image = File:豊鹿嶋神社正面より.jpg | location = [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]] Imokubo 1-2067<ref name="cho"/> | coordinates = {{Coord|35.75791666666667|N|139.41283333333334|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | Status = {{ill|Gōsha (Shinto)|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha (Shinto)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Established = 1466 | Festival = | Map = Japan Tokyo | location = {{ill|Higashiyamato|WD=Q390788}} }}
'''Toyokashima Shrine''' (豊鹿嶋神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato, Tokyo]], Tokyo. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|武御加豆智命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="cho">{{Cite web|ja|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/kitatama/higashiyamato/5762/|title=豊鹿嶋神社|publisher={{ill|Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|東京都神社庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Association of Shinto Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社庁|1=Jinja-cho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590703}}|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>. The shrine's '''main hall (honden)''', built in 1466 during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], is the oldest existing [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto shrine architecture]] in Tokyo<ref name="cho"/>.
== History ==
According to the shrine tradition, in '''707 CE''' ([[:en:Keiun|Keiun]] 4), a '''demon god''' that arrived in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] was pacified at '''Hitachi Peak''', and the shrine was founded by the '''fourth princess of [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]''' and [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]]<ref name="book">{{Cite book |ja|author={{ill|Ueda Mōshin|ja|植田孟縉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōshin|1=Ueda Mōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540684}}|year=1993|title=武蔵名勝図会 |page= |publisher=慶友社|isbn=4874490808}}</ref>. Since then, it has been protected by the residents of the '''Imokubo area''' as their guardian shrine<ref name="cho"/>.
During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was granted '''13 koku''' of shrine land ({{ill|Sharyō|ja|社領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sharyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 寺社領|1=jisha-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11458058}})<ref name="city">{{Cite web|ja|title=11 豊鹿島神社|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,5862,360,622,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-27}}</ref>.
In '''1993''' ([[:en:Heisei era|Heisei era]] 5), during a major '''restoration of the haiden''' (worship hall), a '''tōsatsu (ridge plaque)''' dated to '''1466''' ([[:en:Bunshō|Bunshō]] 1) was discovered, confirming the shrine's founding year, as previously recorded in the '''Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō''' ({{sfn|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}). Additionally, five other '''tōsatsu''' were found, revealing that the haiden before the restoration was constructed in '''1858''' ([[:en:Ansei|Ansei]] 5)<ref name="book"/>.
== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|1=fukudo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630077}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|1=list of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525192}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|1=List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525325}}

== Cultural Properties ==
=== Tokyo Designated Tangible Cultural Property (Architecture) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Honden (Main Hall), 1 building''' - designated as a Tokyo Important Cultural Property on '''November 21, 1964''' (Showa 39), reclassified on '''July 1, 1976'''<ref name="to">{{Cite web|ja|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=205|title=豊鹿島神社本殿|website=Tokyo Cultural Property Database|publisher=[[:en:Tokyo Metropolitan Government Board of Education|Tokyo Metropolitan Board of Education]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : The shrine features a '''single-bay nagare-zukuri''' ([[:en:Nagare-zukuri|Nagare-zukuri]]) main hall with '''kokera-buki (wooden shingles)''' and a '''box ridge'''. The dimensions are '''170.7 cm in width''', with the '''shrine core (moya)''' having a '''depth of 152.4 cm''', and the '''front eaves (kōhai)''' extending '''140.4 cm'''<ref name="to"/>. The structure is currently housed within a '''protective covering hall''' ({{ill|Oudou|ja|覆堂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oudou|1=fukudo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630077}}) to shield it from the elements, making it inaccessible for direct viewing<ref name="cho"/>. Previously, the '''tōsatsu''' stored inside the main hall was believed to indicate the construction year as '''1550''' ({{ill|Tenbun|ja|天文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenbun|1=Tenbun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21096812}} 19), but a '''new tōsatsu''' discovered during the '''1992–1994 dismantling repairs''' confirmed the construction date as '''1466''' (Bunshō 1), with records of a '''master carpenter named Jirōsaburō Chikayoshi'''. The roof was also originally made of '''thick wooden planks (atsuita-buki)'''<ref name="to"/>.
=== Higashiyamato City Designated Cultural Property (Municipal Treasure) ===
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Wooden Komainu (Guardian Lion-Dogs)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983''' (Showa 58)<ref name="bunka">{{Cite web|ja|title=東大和市の文化財|url=https://www.city.higashiyamato.lg.jp/index.cfm/34,2279,359,html|publisher=[[:en:Higashiyamato, Tokyo|Higashiyamato City]]|accessdate=2019-09-28}}</ref> : These wooden komainu are believed to have been crafted '''during the same period as the honden'''<ref name="bunka"/>.
* '''Toyokashima Shrine Shishigashira (Lion Head)''' - designated on '''March 1, 1983'''<ref name="bunka"/> : This lion head, likely from the '''late Edo period''', was used in '''lion dances''' ([[:en:Lion dance|Shishimai]]) that were formerly performed at the shrine<ref name="bunka"/>.
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{cite book|ja|title=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō|volume=Volume 120, Tama District No. 32|chapter=Imokubo Village Kashima Shrine|id={{NDLJP|763994/79}}|publisher={{ill|Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja|内務省地理局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Ministry of Home Affairs|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki Kō}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|ja|東京都の神社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|1=list of Shinto shrines in Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525192}} * {{ill|List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|ja|東京都指定文化財一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|1=List of Tokyo-designated cultural properties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525325}}
